records.ety: [OE. indispos indisposed, feeble, or F. indispos indisposed. See In- not, and Dispose.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Indisposed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Indisposing.]

defs: To render unfit or unsuited; to disqualify.
To disorder slightly as regards health; to make somewhat.
quotes: It made him rather indisposed than sick.

defs: To disincline; to render averse or unfavorable; as, a love of pleasure indisposes the mind to severe study; the pride and selfishness of men indispose them to religious duties.
quotes: The king was sufficiently indisposed towards the persons, or the principles, of Calvin's disciples.
